coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, bacteria infected wound healing is facing some challenges, like production of bacteriotoxin, formation of biofilm and accelerated inflammation in wound. Antibacterial nanomaterials emerge as a potential therapeutic agent for their multiple therapeutic effects and appreciated properties. Herein, a kind of polymer nanoparticles, e.g. H10P10000NPs crosslinked by polyethyleneimine and hydroquinone, were put forward to selectively kill Gram-positive bacteria with good biocompatibility. Meanwhile, H10P10000NPs selectively stained Gram-positive bacteria because of the brilliant green fluorescence, which was supposed to discriminate Gram-positive and -negative bacteria by fluorescence imaging. Additionally, H10P10000NPs could inhibit formation of biofilm and eradicate mature biofilm, which was potential to reduce drug resistance. Furthermore, H10P10000NPs exerted excellent antioxidative ability to eliminate excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) to reduce inflammatory response, which promoted the healing of the wound. When H10P10000NPs were applied to treat the mouse with the skin wound infected by Staphylococcus aureus, accelerated healing of infected wound was observed compared to the positive control. The present study provides a novel idea for the design of “all-in-one” nano agents with selective antibacterial, antibiofilm and ROS scavenging activity for the therapy of bacteria infected wound.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimization of synthetic condition of HnPmNPs. (A) The antibacterial activity of the HnPmNPs synthesized from PEI with different molecular weights (PEI600, PEI1800 and PEI10000). (B) The antibacterial activity of the HnPmNPs with different amounts of HQ (5 mg, 10 mg and 15 mg).

Characterization of the H10P10000NPs. (A) TEM image of the H10P10000NPs and particle size distribution (inset). (B) Fluorescence spectrum. Inset: H10P10000NPs in aqueous under visible light (left) and UV light (λ = 365 nm) (right). (C) UV–vis and (D) FTIR spectra of the H10P10000NPs, HQ and PEI. (E) XPS scan spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (F) C1s, (G) N1s and (H) O1s.

Selective antibacterial activity of H10P10000NPs. Colonies of (A) S. aureus and (B) E. coli and corresponding bacterial survival rates of (C) S. aureus and (D) E. coli treated with H10P10000NPs of different concentrations and CS (100 μg/mL).

Study of the antibacterial mechanism. Fluorescent images of S. aureus (A, C) with and (B, D) without treating by the H10P10000NPs. SEM images of S. aureus (E) with and (F) without treating by the H10P10000NPs (red arrow: cell holes, blue arrow: cell content).
The CLSM images of (A-C) S. aureus and (E-G) E. coli treated with H10P10000NPs. The flow cytometric fluorescence histograms of (D) S. aureus and (H) E. coli treated with H10P10000NPs.

Antibiofilm activity of H10P10000NPs. (AI-DI) Inhibition on the formation of S. aureus biofilms by H10P10000NPs. The live bacteria were stained by Calcein (λex = 488 nm). (EII-PII) Eradication of mature S. aureus biofilms by H10P10000NPs. The dead bacteria were stained by PI (EII-HII) (λex = 550 nm) and H10P10000NPs (III-LII) (λex = 488 nm) and corresponding merged images (MII-PII). The CV assay results of the H10P10000NPs in (R) preventing formation of S. aureus biofilms and (S) eradication of mature S. aureus biofilm.

Antioxidant activity of the H10P10000NPs. The (A, B) ABTS, (D, E) MB and (G, H) NBT assays with the increasing H10P10000NPs concentrations. Scavenging rates of (C) ABTS•+, (F) •OH, (I) O2 •− by the H10P10000NPs. (J-N) The fluorescent photo and scavenging rates of intracellular ROS (O).

In vivo wound healing by the H10P10000NPs. (A) Photos of the wound treated with saline, CS and the H10P10000NPs at specific time points. (B) Schematic illustration of wounds at specific time points corresponding to (A). (C) Photos of bacterial colonies formed on the LB-agar plates derived from the homogenized tissue dispersions of the infected sites of mice treated with saline, CS and H10P10000NPs, respectively. (D) Relative wound and (E) relative body weight of mice at specific time points. (F) H&E and Masson-stained images of wound tissue.

Biocompatibility evaluation of the H10P10000NPs. (A) CCK-8 results of cytotoxicity assessment of the H10P10000NPs. (B) Hemolysis rate of RBCs with the treatment of the H10P10000NPs of different concentrations, saline and H2O. (C) Major organ tissue sections stained with H&E.
Synthesis process and application of H10P10000NPs in wound healing

head.ce:title: Polymer nanoparticles crosslinked by polyethyleneimine and hydroquinone with the selective antibacterial, antibiofilm and antioxidative activity to promote infected wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, bacteria infected wound healing is facing some challenges, like production of bacteriotoxin, formation of biofilm and accelerated inflammation in wound. Antibacterial nanomaterials emerge as a potential therapeutic agent for their multiple therapeutic effects and appreciated properties. Herein, a kind of polymer nanoparticles, e.g. H10P10000NPs crosslinked by polyethyleneimine and hydroquinone, were put forward to selectively kill Gram-positive bacteria with good biocompatibility. Meanwhile, H10P10000NPs selectively stained Gram-positive bacteria because of the brilliant green fluorescence, which was supposed to discriminate Gram-positive and -negative bacteria by fluorescence imaging. Additionally, H10P10000NPs could inhibit formation of biofilm and eradicate mature biofilm, which was potential to reduce drug resistance. Furthermore, H10P10000NPs exerted excellent antioxidative ability to eliminate excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) to reduce inflammatory response, which promoted the healing of the wound. When H10P10000NPs were applied to treat the mouse with the skin wound infected by Staphylococcus aureus, accelerated healing of infected wound was observed compared to the positive control. The present study provides a novel idea for the design of “all-in-one” nano agents with selective antibacterial, antibiofilm and ROS scavenging activity for the therapy of bacteria infected wound.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: On request, the corresponding author will provide the data that back up the study's conclusions. Due to privacy or ethical constraints, the data are not publicly available.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin, the layer of tissue that insulates the body from the external environment to protect the body from surrounding stimuli, has active immune defense, surveillance and self-stabilization functions. Skin can also excrete human waste, sense and regulate body temperature as well [1,2]. However, skin damage is very common in our daily life, such as high temperature injury (e.g. fire burn and empyrosis) and mechanical injury (e.g. knife and puncture wound) [3]. Since the skin injury is often accompanied by the damage of the skin mucous membrane, the wound would become susceptible to germ infection, making it more difficult to heal [4,5], such as Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) [6] and Clostridium tetani infection [7]. If the bacteria in the wound are not eliminated in time, they would secrete bacterial toxin, delay wound healing, deteriorate the wound or even cause death [8]. Additionally, the bacteria infection can also cause inflammation and accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as H2O2, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anion radicals (O2 •−), which are harmful to normal cells [9].

Antibiotics are classical clinical drugs for antibacterial purpose. Although antibiotics demonstrate their effectiveness in most cases, they are losing their priority in killing bacteria due to the well-known drug resistance of bacteria. This poses a serious threat to the global public health, like the emergence of super bacteria [10,11]. Additionally, bacteria can form biofilms, and thus bacterial community may be surrounded by the biofilms, which perhaps protect them from the external drug and resist action of antibiotics [12,13]. Hence, it is of great significance to develop effective medicine with antibacterial and antibiofilm efficacy to overcome the drug resistance of bacteria and further promote the healing of the infected wounds.

In recent decades, nanomedicine boosted rapidly, owing to the appealing properties, like high biocompatibility, quantum confinement effect and controlled diversified bioactivities [14,15], etc. Up to now, various nanomaterials have been developed for the antibacterial purpose, including metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [16], carbon-based nanomaterials [14], metal-based nanomaterials [17,18], polymer-based nanomaterials [19], liposomes [20], silicon-based nanomaterials [15], et al. These materials demonstrated their effectiveness for antibacterial purpose. However, some problems are still to be addressed. For example, biocompatibility of MOFs and metal based nanomaterials was always questioned, because of the involvement of metals [21]. Similarly, degradability and clearance of silica-based materials hampered their applications in vivo [22]. The reproducible high-yield production of carbon-based materials was in some cases difficult, making the real application of this kind of material impossible [14]. Undoubtedly, liposomes have good biocompatibility and reproducible mass production, but the release of antimicrobial agents from liposomes is an inevitable problem [20]. Among them, polymer based materials could be designed on purpose with mass production and antibacterial ability in nature; Degradation and clearance could be possible. From this respect, the polymer based materials were one of the promising antibacterial agent.

The antibacterial mechanisms of nanomaterials are different from conventional antibiotics, such as nanozymes (e.g. peroxidase and oxidase-like enzyme activity) [23], photodynamic therapy [11], photothermal effect [24], destruction of cell membrane and cell wall [25,26], affection of the genetic process [27] and so forth. In addition to developing various nanomaterials with diverse antibacterial mechanisms, of course, designing nanomedicine with selective antimicrobial activity is another potential strategy to deduce the emergence of drug resistance [28]. Although broad-spectrum antibacterial materials usually possess remarkable antibacterial activity for multiple bacterial infections, their effects were transient and they may mess up the delicate balance of microbial flora at the infection site after a long-term use, resulting in unanticipated and undesirable consequences, like drug resistance and side effects [28,29]. To address the problem caused by nonspecific bactericidal action, strain-selective bactericidal materials came into being [30,31]. Aiming at this goal, antibiotics which possessed the selective antimicrobial activity were in some cases to be loaded or modified onto nanomaterials [32,33]. For instance, vancomycin was once loaded on silicon nanoparticles to target S. aureus or multidrug-resistant bacteria [32,33]. Alternatively, modification with some aptamers onto the nanomaterials could be also effective to assist the materials target specific bacteria [34]. For example, cyclic 9-amino-acid peptide could specifically target S. aureus [34]. Anyway, it was seldom that the nanomaterials possessed selective antibacterial ability themselves.

Wound healing is accomplished through cell regeneration and migration, but this process is often accompanied by inflammation, especially in infected wounds. In the inflammatory response, a common phenomenon is the production of excessive ROS, which is harmful to the proteins and normal cells, thus slowing down the wound healing [12,13]. Hence, it is an effective way to scavenge redundant ROS in the wound to reduce inflammatory response and further promote wound healing. Consequently, developing nanomaterials which combine antibacterial ability and antioxidant activity for the therapy of germ infection is challenging but significant in the field of nano antibacterial agents.

In this study, a kind of nanomaterials, namely H10P10000NPs, with the selective antibacterial ability, antibiofilm and excellent ROS scavenging ability, were proposed. Unlike other familiar fluorescent nanomaterial synthesized under harsh conditions, H10P10000NPs with strong fluorescence were directly crosslinked by polyethyleneimine (PEI) and hydroquinone (HQ) at room temperature through simple stirring (Scheme 1 ). PEI was usually applied to synthesize or modify nanomaterials for the antibacterial purpose because of its abundant amino groups (primary, secondary and tertiary amines), which can endow the materials with positive charges, while its long alkyl chain can provide hydrophobicity to the materials. Both the positively charged surface and hydrophobicity can facilitate the interaction between the materials and bacteria, and penetration of materials into the bacterial cell. Meanwhile, HQ was reported to have a selective antibacterial property against Gram-positive bacteria [35–37]. Thus, the combination of HQ and PEI exerted excellent selective antibacterial activity towards Gram-positive bacteria but not Gram-negative ones. The selective antibacterial activity of H10P10000NPs could be due to the synergistic effect of different penetration rates of materials into different bacteria and the selective antibacterial ability of HQ. Moreover, H10P10000NPs were demonstrated to inhibit the formation of S. aureus biofilm and destroy mature S. aureus biofilm, which may further reduce drug resistance. In addition, H10P10000NPs could scavenge ROS, possibly related to reducibility of both PEI and HQ. Another interesting finding was that H10P10000NPs could selectively stain the Gram-positive bacteria with remarkable green fluorescence, but not Gram-negative bacteria, which held the potential to distinguish Gram-positive and -negative bacteria. As a result, H10P10000NPs succeeded in treating the mouse with the skin wound infected by S. aureus with favorable therapeutic effect. This work provided a novel idea to design “all-in-one” nanomaterials for the therapy of bacteria infected wound.

PEI of different molecular weights (Mw = 600, 1800, 10,000), riboflavin, methionine (Met), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), 2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS), methylene blue (MB), 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and crystal violet (CV) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HQ, ethanol, NaCl, Na2HPO4•12H2O, NaH2PO4•3H2O and H2O2 were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Agar powder, yeast extract and tryptone were commercially available from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Calcein acetoxymethyl ester (Calcein-AM)/ propidium iodide (PI) reagent test kits were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cefquinome sulfate (CS) was bought from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells and normal human kidney epithelial 293 T cells were provided by the China Center for Type Culture Collection (Wuhan, China).

A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Japan) was applied to observe the morphology of materials. Fluorescence images were observed by a DP73 fluorescence microscope (OLYMPUS, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were carried out on an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The fluorescence spectra and intensity were measured by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) with the excitation and emission slit of 10 nm. Ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) absorbance was measured on an UV-1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The confocal photos were taken by a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Nikon, Japan). The flow cytometer was BD Acurri C6 (BD, USA). Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were taken by a field emission SEM (MIRA 3, TESCAN Brno, s.r.o., Russia). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Zeta potential of materials was measured by a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, England). The contact angles were determined by a DSA100S droplet shape analyzer (KRÜSS, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polymer nanoparticles crosslinked by HQ and PEI
ce:section.ce:para: The polymer nanoparticles crosslinked by HQ and PEI were prepared by a green method under mild conditions as reported previously [38,39]. Briefly, HQ of certain amount (5, 10 or 15 mg) was added into 6 mL of 0.1 g/mL PEI of specific molecular weight (Mw = 600, 1800, 10,000) aqueous solution with stirring for 6 h. After that, the solution was transferred into a 14,000 Da dialysis bag and dialyzed against deionized water for a week with renewing water every 24 h. The resulting materials were denoted as HnPmNPs, where subscript n refers to the amount of HQ and m refers to the molecular weight of PEI. The antibacterial activity of HnPmNPs against S. aureus was chosen as the criterion to optimize the synthesis of the nanoparticles.

The Gram-positive (S. aureus, Enterococcus faecium (E. faecalis), Streptococcus pneumoniae (S. pneumoniae) and Enterococcus faecium (E. faecium)) and Gram-negative (Escherichia coli (E. coli), Aeromonas hydrophila (A. hydrophila), Pseudomonas rhodesiae (P. rhodesiae) and Enterobacter cloacae (E. cloacae)) bacteria were cultured with the Luria-Bertani (LB) medium overnight to reach the logarithmic growth period. The antibacterial efficiency was evaluated through plate count method. Briefly, Gram-positive or Gram-negative strain (1 × 106 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL) was mixed with H10P10000NPs of varied concentrations or CS (100 μg/mL) as positive control in PBS (pH 5.5) with shaking for 4 h at 37 °C, and then diluted 50 times with PBS. This diluted dispersion (50 μL) was then smeared evenly on LB agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for another 18 h. Finally, the bacterial colonies were photographed and counted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CLSM observation and flow cytometric analysis of bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Each kind of Gram-positive or Gram-negative bacteria was co-incubated with H10P10000NPs (1 mg/mL) for 4 h at 37 °C. The bacteria were then collected after centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 3 min, and washed by PBS in triple. Thus-obtained bacteria were subjected to CLSM observation and flow cytometric analysis. As for flow cytometric analysis, the statistical number of 10,000, the channel of FL1 (green fluorescence) and fast flow rate were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The live/dead staining assay and SEM observation
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus (∼108 CFU/mL, 200 μL) and 200 μL H10P10000NPs (1 mg/mL) were co-incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Afterwards, the bacteria were washed by PBS (pH 5.5) for three times. For the live/dead staining assay, the bacteria were stained by a Calcein-AM/PI reagent test kit according to the instruction, and then photographed by an inverted fluorescence microscope. For the SEM observation, the bacteria were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde at 4 °C overnight, dehydrated gradually using 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, and 90% ethanol solution in this order for every 15 min, and re-suspended in the absolute ethanol. The bacteria were then sampled and observed by the SEM microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition on the formation of biofilm by H10P10000NPs
ce:section.ce:para: S. aures was propagated in the Tryptic Soy Broth (TSB) medium with H10P10000NPs of varied concentrations and incubated in the 96-well plate for 24 h at 37 °C without shaking. The medium was then sucked out, and the well with the biofilm attached to the bottom was washed by PBS. Afterwards, the CV was used to stain the biofilm [12,13]. Briefly, 200 μL of the CV solution (0.2%) was added to the well for incubation at room temperature. After 30 min, the solution was decanted, and the well was added with PBS for washing twice. After the addition of 0.2 mL of 95% ethanol to each well, the optical density at 590 nm (OD590) was measured in order to quantify the residual biofilms.

To study the morphology of the biofilm, biofilms were directly cultured in the confocal dish and stained with Calcein, keeping other operations the same as the above experimental step, but followed by CLSM observations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Eradication of mature biofilm by H10P10000NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The mature biofilm was obtained by culturing S. aureus in the TSB medium for 24 h. Thus-obtained mature biofilms were then incubated in PBS as the control group and co-incubated with H10P10000NPs of varied concentrations as the experimental groups for 4 h. The biofilms were observed through the CLSM and quantified using the CV assay as described in Section 2.3.4, except that biofilms were stained with PI.

The ABTS, MB and NBT assays were adopted to evaluate the scavenging ability of H10P10000NPs towards total free radicals, •OH and O2 •−, respectively. For the ABTS assay, 38.4 mg ABTS and 6.59 mg K2S2O8 were dissolved in 10 mL of water and kept in dark to generate ABTS•+. This solution was then diluted to the appropriate concentration with the absorbance at 734 nm between 0.6 and 0.9, with the addition of H10P10000NPs of different concentrations (the experimental group) or water (the control group). After keeping in dark for 30 min, the solution was subjected to the absorbance determination (λ = 734 nm) by a microplate reader, and the ABTS•+ consuming rate of H10P10000NPs was calculated based on Eq. (1):

Where ACG is the absorbance of the control group, AC0 is the absorbance of the solvent, AEG means the absorbance of the experimental group, and AE0 represents the absorbance of H10P10000NPs of specific concentration in the corresponding water or PB.

For the NBT assay, 13 mM Met, 75 μM NBT, 20 μM riboflavin and H10P10000NPs of varied concentrations in 50 mM PB (pH 7.8) were mixed and subjected to an LED flat panel luminary (100 W) irradiation for 5 min. The sterile distilled water of the same volume was taken as the control group. The absorbance of the mixtures was determined (λ = 560 nm) and the O2 •− consuming rate of H10P10000NPs was also calculated by Eq. (1).

For the MB assay, 250 μM H2O2, 25 μM MB and 500 μM Fe2+ were mixed with H10P10000NPs of specific concentration as the experimental group. The same volume of water instead of H10P10000NPs was used as the control group (I). For the control group (II), the same volume of water instead of both H2O2 and H10P10000NPs was used. After incubating for 30 min, the absorbance (λ = 664 nm) of the solutions was determined and the •OH scavenging ability of H10P10000NPs was evaluated by Eq. (2):

Where AMG means the absorbance of the experimental group, ACGI is absorbance of the control group (I), ACGII is the absorbance of the control group (II).

H2O2 was applied to simulate the excessive ROS in cells. Typically, SHSY-5Y cells were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated in the Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) (∼8000 cells per well) at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then treated by only 150 μM H2O2 (the positive control), 150 μM H2O2 and H10P10000NPs of varied concentrations (the experimental group), or without any treatment (the control group), respectively. After 4 h incubation, the cells were washed by PBS (pH = 7.4) twice, and then added with 200 μL of DFCH-DA (DMEM, 10 mM). After incubated for another 30 min, the cells were gently washed by the DMEM for twice and re-immersed in 100 μL DMEM, subjected to the observation by an inversed fluorescence microscopy.

The CCK-8 (Cell Counting Kit-8) assay was employed to evaluate the cell cytotoxicity of H10P10000NPs through the viability of the normal human kidney epithelial cells, i.e. 293 T cells. Briefly, 293 T cells were incubated in 96-well plates (DMEM medium, ∼1 × 104 cells per well) at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h, and then H10P10000NPs of different concentrations were added with incubation for another 24 h. Cell viability was determined by the CCK-8 kit.

The fresh blood was collected from Balb/c mouse. Red blood cells (RBCs) were collected by centrifuging the fresh blood at 1200 rpm for 15 min and washed 3 times with normal saline (0.9%), and then diluted in normal saline to the concentration of 2% (v/v), and the blood cells number was checked by hematimetry. The H10P10000NPs obtained in Section 2.2 was diluted with normal saline to the desired concentrations and mixed with the above RBCs solution (1:1, v/v), using deionized water and normal saline as positive and negative control respectively. After incubation for 4 h at 37 °C, 100 μL of the supernatant was collected by centrifugation and transferred to another 96-well plate, and the OD value at 540 nm was determined by a microplate reader. The hemolysis rate was calculated using Eq. (3):
(3)

Animal experiments were approved by Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology (SYXK 2021–0057). Female Balb/c mice (7–8 weeks old) were obtained from the Experimental Animal Research Center of Hubei province. An approximately cutaneous wound with a diameter of 8 mm was created by surgical scissors using a ring mold of 8 mm internal diameter on the back of each mouse. After that, 50 μL of S. aureus pathogen (∼109 CFU) was dropped on the wounds of each mouse, which were fed normally overnight to establish an infected wound model. The mice were randomly divided into three groups (I), (II) and (III) with different treatments by (I) saline (50 μL), (II) H10P10000NPs (50 μL, 10 mg/mL) and (III) CS (50 μL, 10 mg/mL). To evaluate the antibacterial effect of H10P10000NPs on infected wound, the wound tissue was harvested, homogenized in normal saline solution on the first day after treatment. The diluted homogenates (50 times) were coated on the LB solid medium for incubation at 37 °C, and colonies were counted after 18 h. The wounds of the mice were photographed, and their weights were recorded daily. After seven days, the mice were sacrificed. The wound tissues and major organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, were harvested for the hematoxylin-eosin staining (H&E) and Masson staining.

Each experiment was performed at least three times in parallel. All data were presented as mean values ± SD. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was utilized to analyze the statistical significance between different treatment groups (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, n.s. represents no significant difference).

As reported [38,39], HnPmNPs were formed mainly based on the Schiff base reaction, as depicted in Scheme 2 . At first, HQ is easily oxidized to p-benzoquinone in a basic condition, which could be provided by the other reactant, PEI herein. Secondly, the primary amino on PEI would react with the carbonyl of p-benzoquinone, experiencing dehydration and formation of -C=N. In this way, PEI was cross-linked with HQ, producing nanoparticles. Compared with other methods to prepare nanomaterials, like hydrothermal, pyrolysis and microwave, etc., this reaction was simple, green and mild, and processed with only two reactants at room temperature by stirring without extra reagent and external energy.

In order to obtain the materials with the optimal antibacterial activity, the two main factors that may influence this property of the materials were investigated, e.g. molecular weight of PEI and the amount of HQ. As depicted in Fig. 1A, with the increasing molecular weight of PEI from 600 to 10,000, the survival rates of the S. aureus treated separately with H10P600NPs, H10P1800NPs and H10P10000NPs were decreased in this order. The surface charge and hydrophobicity of materials could influence the interaction between the materials and bacteria, and thus penetration of the materials into bacteria [33]. The zeta potentials of H10P600NPs, H10P1800NPs and H10P10000NPs were determined as +21.83 ± 0.47, +21.33 ± 0.75 and + 22.27 ± 0.68 eV, respectively, which were not significantly different. Besides, the water contact angles of H10P600NPs, H10P1800NPs and H10P10000NPs increased from 21°, 31° to 45° (Fig. S1, supporting information), reflecting the elevated hydrophobicity. With the increasing molecular weight, PEI owned longer alkyl chain, which would entail high hydrophobicity to PEI [40]. Among the three studied PEI, i.e. PEI600, PEI1800 and PEI10000, PEI10000 with the longest alkyl chain generated the most hydrophobic H10P10000NPs. It has been reported that the enhanced hydrophobicity of the antibacterial materials would facilitate the transmembrane progress of themselves, and thus promote the penetration rate of the materials [41]. Hence, H10P10000NPs were easier to penetrate the bacteria, exhibiting the strongest antibacterial activity, largely due to its relatively high hydrophobicity.

Furthermore, the effect of the amount of HQ on the antibacterial activity of HnPmNPs was investigated at the fixed amount of PEI10000 (Fig. 1B). The best antibacterial activity was obtained in the case of H10P10000NPs. The contact angles of the three materials were all measured to be ∼45°, indicating the comparable hydrophobicity of them. The zeta potentials were + 21.23 ± 0.71 eV, +22.27 ± 0.68 eV and + 13.77 ± 0.80 eV for H5P10000NPs, H10P10000NPs and H15P10000NPs, respectively, while S. aureus was −12.23 ± 0.25 eV and E. coli was −8.03 ± 0.22 eV. H5P10000NPs and H10P10000NPs possessed almost identical zeta potentials, but the latter behaved stronger antibacterial activity, which may be ascribed to the enhanced amount of HQ with satisfactory antibacterial activity against S. aureus [35–37]. When the amount of HQ was increased to a certain value, 15 mg in the case (for H15P10000NPs), the zeta potential of the resulting materials decreased distinctly, which may weaken the electrostatic interaction between the materials and negatively charged bacteria even more. Although further enhanced HQ may promote antibacterial activity, the antibacterial activity of H15P10000NPs became weakened as a comprehensive result. To sum up, the optimal synthetic condition was that 10 mg HQ was dissolved in 6 mL of 0.1 g/mL PEI10000 with stirring for 6 h, yielding H10P10000NPs.

In order to study the formation and composition of H10P10000NPs, the UV–Vis, FTIR and XPS spectra of H10P10000NPs were studied. As depicted in Fig. 2C, compared to PEI and HQ, H10P10000NPs possessed a broad absorption peak centering at ∼375 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition of the CN, while the absorption at 290 nm (π-π*, benzene ring) diminished [38,39]. In the FTIR spectrum of H10P10000NPs (Fig. 2D), a new band of -C=N/-C=O overlapping absorption (1657 cm−1) occurred, which further confirmed the crosslinking of PEI and HQ. Comparing the FTIR spectra of PEI and H10P10000NPs, they both exhibited the symmetrical and asymmetrical -CH2 stretching vibrations (2935 and 2860 cm−1) and NH bending vibrations (1567 cm−1). Meanwhile, both H10P10000NPs and HQ showed vibration of benzene structure (1476 and 1365 cm−1) and bending vibration of CO (1270 cm−1) [38,39]. The existence of these peaks demonstrated that some relevant functional groups from the raw materials, such as amino, phenolic hydroxyl and benzene ring, were reserved in H10P10000NPs.

As displayed in Fig. 2E, three main peaks, i.e. O1s (531.5 eV, 7.15%), N1s (409.9 eV, 18.72%) and C1s (297.9 eV, 74.13%), were found for H10P10000NPs. The C1s profile displayed four obvious characteristic peaks at 284.1, 284.7, 285.6 and 287.3 eV, corresponding to C − C/C=C, C − N, CO and CO /C=N, respectively (Fig. 2F) [42,43]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum revealed three peaks at 398.5, 399 and 400.2 eV, which were ascribed to -NH, -C=N and C-N-C, respectively (Fig. 2G) [44–46]. The peaks in the O1s spectrum at 531.3 and 531.8 eV were assigned to CO and C-OH, respectively (Fig. 2H) [47]. From the XPS results, some functional groups of raw materials, including amino, phenolic hydroxyl and benzene ring, were reserved in H10P10000NPs, similar to the results in UV–Vis and FTIR spectra.

The antibacterial activity of H10P10000NPs against the typical Gram-positive and -negative bacteria, i.e. S. aureus and E. coli, was systematically studied. As photographed in Fig. 3A and B, H10P10000NPs exerted favorable selective antibacterial activity against S. aureus, while nearly no lethality to E. coli. Specifically, H10P10000NPs could almost completely kill S. aureus at the concentration of 80 μg/mL (Fig. 3C), but had no effectiveness against E. coli even at a high concentration up to 640 μg/mL (Fig. 3D). In addition, H10P10000NPs exerted stronger antibacterial activity against S. aureus than CS which is a fourth-generation cephalosporin for animals. This pronounced selective antibacterial activity of H10P10000NPs against S. aureus could be explained as follows. On one hand, HQ was claimed to possess appropriate antibacterial activity against S. aureus but no lethality against E. coli, possibly due to its morphological tridimensional structure [35–37]. As discussed above, some functional groups, such as phenolic hydroxyl and benzene ring groups of HQ, were retained in H10P10000NPs, which may endow H10P10000NPs with antibacterial selectivity. However, the antibacterial activity of H10P10000NPs is much stronger than that of HQ, which could only kill 6.02% of S. aureus at the concentration of 100 μg/mL from the plate count experimental result (data not shown). The enhanced antibacterial activity of H10P10000NPs may be attributed to the synergistic effect of HQ and PEI which possessed excellent antibacterial activity according to our previous research [48]. On the other hand, the different penetration rates of antibacterial materials into bacteria may also contribute to the selective antibacterial activity. Gram-positive bacteria, like S. aureus, whose surface mostly contains negatively charged teichoic acids and a thick coating of peptidoglycan [44–46], are more susceptible for the penetration of H10P10000NP with positive charges. In contrast, Gram-negative bacteria, like E. coli, have another outer membrane linked to the cell wall by lipopolysaccharide, which would create extra barrier for the materials to enter [44–46]. Depending on this difference of bacterial structure, some nano antibacterial agents exerted selective antibacterial activity, such as N,N-dimethyl-N-octadecylbetaine modified silicon nanoparticles [44], self-assembled fiber-like micelles from diblock copolymers modified with cysteamine hydrochloride [45] and carbon dots derived from folic acid [46]. Compared with these nano agents, the H10P10000NPs possessed not only different penetration rates to different types of bacteria, but also the selective antibacterial activity itself. Hence, in addition to the different membrane/wall constructure of Gram-positive and -negative bacteria [44–46], choosing raw materials that possessed selectively kill bacteria or functional groups that could recognize bacteria [32,49] to construct the nano agents may be a useful strategy for selective antibacterial activity.

To further verify this specific antibacterial activity of H10P10000NPs, the other three Gram-positive bacteria, e.g. E. faecalis, S. pneumoniae and E. faecium, and three additional Gram-negative bacteria, e.g. A. hydrophila, P. rhodesiae and E. cloacae, were randomly chosen for antibacterial experiments (Fig. S2, supporting information). According to the plate count experimental result, H10P1000NPs demonstrated strong antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria at low concentrations, with the survival rates of E. faecalis, S. pneumoniae and E. faecium at 19.57% ± 2.77% (80 μg/mL), 0% (40 μg/mL) and 17.51% ± 3.57% (80 μg/mL), respectively. However, poor antibacterial activity against Gram-negative bacteria was obtained even at a high concentration of H10P1000NPs up to 640 μg/mL, with the survival rates of A. hydrophila, P. rhodesiae and E. cloacae at 87.98% ± 4.28%, 80.05% ± 4.35% and 70.96% ± 2.60%, respectively. These results fully demonstrate the selective antibacterial activity of the H10P10000NPs towards Gram-positive bacteria.

The live/dead staining assay was further performed to study the antibacterial mechanism of H10P10000NPs by the live/dead staining kit, which was comprised of PI and Calcein-AM. The PI can only enter the dead cell with destructed membrane and combine with nucleic acid, turning on the red fluorescence in the bacteria, while the Calcein-AM can enter the live cell with integrated cell membrane and be enzymolysised by esterase, thus turning on green fluorescence. As illustrated in Fig. 4A and C, S
. aureus without treating by the H10P10000NPs exhibited intense green fluorescence and extremely weak red fluorescence, demonstrating the survival status of the bacteria. On the contrary, S. aureus treated with H10P10000NPs emitted both strong green and red fluorescence simultaneously. As demonstrated in Fig. 2B, H10P10000NPs could stain the S. aureus with green fluorescence. Thus, in this case, the green fluorescence came from H10P10000NPs. However, the obviously red fluorescence came from PI (Fig. 4B and D), indicating the dead status of the bacteria, possibly due to the destruction of cell membrane by H10P10000NPs. To verify this deduction, the SEM (Fig. 4E and F) and TEM (Fig. S3) observations were performed. The S. aureus in the control group possessed smooth and intact surface, while the bacteria in the experimental group appeared broken surface, leakage of cellular content and cell debris, and also the same observations from TEM images (Fig. S3). From the above analysis, the selective antibacterial activity of H10P10000NPs might be derived from two aspects. On one hand, H10P10000NPs inherited some functionalities, i.e. benzene ring and phenolic hydroxyl, from HQ, which was claimed to behave selective antibacterial activity [35–37], making H10P10000NPs more sensitive to Gram-positive bacteria. On the other hand, Gram-positive bacteria has monolayer membrane [44–46], and the H10P10000NPs could easily penetrate the bacteria, because of their suitable charges and hydrophobicity, leading to membrane destruction and leakage of cellular contents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective staining of gram-positive bacteria by H10P10000NPs
ce:section.ce:para: H10P10000NPs could emit strong green fluorescence themselves (Fig. 2B), and this property could be utilized to stain the bacteria and thus to study the penetration or interaction between H10P10000NPs and bacteria. As imaged in Fig. 5A-C and E-G, each single S. aureus cell treated with H10P10000NPs emitted strong green fluorescence, while only a few E. coli emitted green fluorescence. This observation intuitively illustrates the different penetration rates of H10P10000NPs into different bacterial cells. Furthermore, as detected by the flow cytometry in Fig. 5D and H, 90.7% of S. aureus were stained by H10P10000NPs, while only 25.4% of E. coli were stained.

The above observations revealed that H10P10000NPs may specifically distinguish Gram-positive bacteria, but seldom stain Gram-negative bacteria. As disclosed in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information), other three kinds of Gram-positive bacteria treated with H10P10000NPs emitted strong green fluorescence, while nearly no fluorescence of the three Gram-negative bacteria treated with H10P10000NPs was observed. As determined by the flow cytometry, the staining rates towards E. faecalis, S. pneumoniae and E. faecium by H10P10000NPs were 86.5%, 91.2% and 60.3%, respectively, while only <5% staining rates were found for the three studied Gram-negative bacteria. These results demonstrate that H10P10000NPs could selectively stain Gram-positive bacteria, making H10P10000NPs suitable for discriminating Gram-positive and -negative bacteria via fluorescence imaging.

Biofilm, a complex community formed by aggregation and growth of a great deal of bacteria within an extracellular polymeric substance, plays a crucial role for bacteria to maintain micro-environmental stability and resist the action of drug [45]. Destroying mature biofilm or inhibiting formation of biofilm could weaken the drug resistance of bacteria and enhance antibacterial effect [12,13]. Hence, it would be the extra merit for the antibacterial materials to inhibit the formation of biofilm and/or eradicate mature biofilm. Herein, the Calcein-AM was utilized to stain the bacteria to observe the formation of S. aures biofilm. As described in Section 2.3.4, if the S. aureus biofilm was formed on the bottom of the confocal dish, the Calcein could stain the live S. aureus in the biofilm and emitted green fluorescence. As imaged in Fig. 6AI, the S. aureus themselves without treating by H10P10000NPs could form dense and thick biofilm. Otherwise, after treated by H10P10000NPs of a high concentration (400 μg/mL, Fig. 6DI), no green fluorescence was observed. This was because the bacteria were washed away by PBS during operation as nearly no biofilm was formed on the bottom of the dish, revealing that H10P10000NPs could inhibit the formation of biofilm. Anyway, the bacteria treated with the H10P10000NPs at the concentration of 100 and 200 μg/mL generated sparse and thin biofilm, and exerted concentration dependent behavior, i.e. the biofilm was sparser with the enhanced concentration of H10P10000NPs (Fig. 6BI-CI ). Moreover, as quantified by the CV assay (Fig. 6R), the formation of the biofilm decreased from 100% to 5% with the increasing concentration of H10P10000NPs from 0 to 400 μg/mL, further demonstrating the favorable ability of the H10P10000NPs to inhibit the formation of biofilm.

In addition to inhibiting the formation of biofilm, the ability of H10P10000NPs to eradicate mature biofilm was also investigated. The difference from the above inhibitory experiment was that, herein, red fluorescent PI was used to tell the dead bacteria in the biofilm, and stronger red fluorescence meant more dead bacteria. As described in Section 2.3.5, S. aureus strains were pre-incubated in confocal dish and the formed biofilm was attached on the bottom of the dish. As imaged in Fig. 6EII, the biofilm without treating by H10P10000NPs only showed sporadic red fluorescence ascribed to the naturally dead bacterial cell. However, after treated by H10P10000NPs, the S. aureus inside were dead and emitted green fluorescence of H10P10000NPs, and were simultaneously stained by PI to emit red fluorescence. Thus, in this case, both green (Fig. 6 JII-LII) and red fluorescence (Fig. 6FII-HII) could be observed; In addition, as merged in Fig. 6 6NII-PII, the biofilm appeared yellow fluorescence, due to the combination of the red fluorescence of PI and green fluorescence of H10P10000NPs, suggesting that H10P10000NPs could kill the S. aureus in the mature biofilm. Also, as quantified by the CV assay (Fig. 6S), with the increasing H10P10000NPs from 0 to 400 μg/mL, the biofilm was destroyed from 0 to 81%, which further confirmed the favorable ability of H10P10000NPs to eradicate the mature biofilm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scavenging ability of H10P10000NPs towards ROS
ce:section.ce:para: ROS, e.g. H2O2, •OH and·O2 •−, may be produced at the bacteria infecting wound, which can be harmful to the proteins and normal cells, and therefore slow down the wound healing. Hence, to alleviate the oxidative stress was regarded as an effective strategy to promote the wound healing [50,51]. Herein, the ABTS, NBT and MB assays were employed to study the scavenging abilities of H10P10000NPs towards total free radicals, •OH and O2 •−, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 7A-C, H10P10000NPs owned excellent scavenging ability towards ABTS•+ with the EC50 (concentration for 50% of maximal effect) of ∼12.5 μg/mL, and the ABTS•+ could be entirely scavenged at the H10P10000NPs concentration of 50 μg/mL. For •OH (Fig. 7D-F), the H10P10000NPs also showed favorable scavenging activity with the EC50 of ∼37 μg/mL, while the •OH could be completely consumed at the H10P10000NPs concentration of 100 μg/mL. However, H10P10000NPs exhibited weaker scavenging ability to O2 •− with the EC50 > 200 μg/mL (Fig. 7G-I). Both PEI and HQ possessed reducibility, for instance, PEI was reported to prepare gold nanoparticles as reduction agent [52], and HQ also exerted ability to scavenge radicals [37]. Additionally, H10P10000NPs inherited some functionalities of PEI and HQ as discussed above. These could be reasons for H10P10000NPs' scavenging activity towards ROS.

Furthermore, the scavenging ability of H10P10000NPs towards intracellular ROS was tested. The control group showed nearly no fluorescence (Fig. 7J) and the cell treated with H2O2 emitted strong green fluorescence of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) (Fig. 7K), while the cell treated with both H2O2 and H10P10000NPs showed weaker fluorescence (Fig. 7L-N). At the studied concentration of H10P10000NPs from 0 to 40 μg/mL, H10P10000NPs could consume the intracellular ROS efficiently up to 98.1%, almost irrelevant of the H10P10000NPs concentration. The consuming rate of intracellular ROS could achieve 90% even at the low concentration down to 10 μg/mL (Fig. 7O). Those results illustrate the excellent scavenging ability of H10P10000NPs against ROS in vitro and in cell, which would be appreciated to remove excessive ROS generated in the wound site to reduce inflammatory response.

Bacteria infected injury can hinder the healing of the wound due to the production of bacterial toxin and inflammatory response. Based on the satisfactory antibacterial and antioxidant ability of H10P10000NPs as discussed above, the applicability of H10P10000NPs to bacteria infected wound healing was herein tested. The S. aureus infected mouse skin wound model was firstly established, and then treated with H10P10000NPs, using 0.9% saline and CS for comparison. Fig. 8A was the macroscopic views of the wound healing in mice back skin at different time intervals, and Fig. 8B provided the schematic depiction of the wound healing procedure for each group for intuitive observation. Obviously, the wound site treated with H10P10000NPs and CS exhibited the accelerated wound healing; after the 7th day infected, scab was falling off and new flesh began to grow. Meanwhile, compared to the positive control (the CS group), the H10P10000NPs group exerted obviously more accelerated wound healing, with the remarkably smaller wound area at the same time intervals. Furthermore, bacterial colonies formed on the LB-agar plates derived from the homogenized infected sites of mice revealed that both CS and H10P10000NPs could kill the bacteria on infected wound, while bacteria were still active in the case of saline (Fig. 8C). After seven days, healing rates of 72.22% ± 2.97%, 69.2% ± 0.628% and 43.16% ± 2.40% were observed for the H10P10000NPs, CS and the saline group, respectively (Fig. 8D). Anyway, the saline group showed the slowest wound healing with the wound scabbed. Additionally, the mice with wounds did not lose any weight within seven days (Fig. 8E), indicating that penetrating H10P10000NPs into the tissue was not hazardous in vivo. These observations demonstrated the effectiveness of both H10P10000NPs and CS as the antibacterial agents to kill S. aureus in the wound site, reducing the damage of bacteria to the wound. Another noticed merit of H10P10000NPs lies in its antioxidant activity, enhanced healing rate could be related to the elimination of excessive ROS.

Skin tissues from the area around the wound was collected for tissue staining study to better understand how H10P10000NPs promoted wound healing. The saline group exhibited incomplete skin tissue with a significant degree of inflammatory cell infiltration, while the CS group showed complete skin with a lighter degree of inflammatory cell infiltration. Contrarily, after treating by H10P10000NPs, inflammatory infiltrates were diminished, and hair follicles rejuvenated, exhibiting superior antimicrobial abilities and wound healing (Fig. 8F), which further proved that H10P10000NPs could reduce the inflammatory response [46]. Additionally, Masson staining was also carried out to observe collagen deposition and dispersion. It revealed that collagen fibers were distributed uniformly in the CS and H10P10000NPs groups as opposed to the saline group, and provided a measurement of the collagen fibers' surface area (Fig. 8F).

Favorable biocompatibility is another crucial precondition for the in vivo application. The biosecurity of H10P10000NPs was thus evaluated by cytotoxicity and hemolysis assays. For the cytotoxicity experiment, the viability of 293 T incubated with H10P10000NPs of varied concentrations was measured by the CCK-8 assay. Basically, the experimental results showed that the cell viability was around 100% for doses from 0 to 40 μg/mL. When the concentration was increased to 80 μg/mL, the relative cell viability was slightly reduced, but >80% cell viability still remained, indicating the favorable biocompatibility of H10P10000NPs (Fig. 9A). In the hemolysis test, blood cells number was checked by hematimetry as 8 × 106 per milliliter, and there was no significant hemolysis was observed for H10P10000NPs (Fig. 9B). These results indicate that H10P10000NPs had good hemocompatibility, which would be appreciated for the in vivo antibacterial and wound healing applications.

In order to examine the pathological changes in major organs, the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of the mice were harvested after the treatment, and subjected to H&E for histopathological analysis. As displayed in Fig. 9C, no lesions were discovered in any studied group, further confirming the biological safety of the treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between reported polymeric nanomaterials and H10P10000NPs for selective antibacteria
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, polymer-based materials won some popularity for antibacterial purpose owing to their inherent properties [19,51]. However, the polymeric nanomaterials for selective antibacteria were still few and developing. The comparison between most recently reported polymeric nanomaterials for selective antibacteria with the present proposed one was made in Table S1. As can be seen from Table S1, the selectivity could be originated from three aspects mainly. Firstly, the different constructure of cell wall and membrane resulted in the different penetration rates of nanomaterials into different types of bacteria [45,53]. Secondly, the nanomaterials derived from raw materials with selective antibacterial activity or ability to recognize bacteria reserved the corresponding selectivity, like glycopolymer poly[2-(methacrylamido)glucopyranose] could specifically recognize E. coli pili protein [54], protein A-antibody could recognize protein A on surface of S. aureus [55], S. aureus aptamer specifically recognize S. aureus and Se4+possessed selectivity antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria [56]. Thirdly, the nanomaterials could react with the specific protein [57] or enzyme [58] to exert specific antibacterial activity. Anyway, from Table S1, we can see that, although most of the reported polymeric nanomaterials had several mechanisms for antibacteria, few had more than one mechanism for antibacterial selectivity. However, our H10P10000NPs were “all-in-one” nano agent, in addition to the selective antibacterial property from the raw reactant (HQ) and different penetration rates to different constructure of cell wall and membrane, antibiofilm and ROS scavenging activity, which were less discussed in reported studies, also contributed to the enhanced antibacterial properties, and thus promote the wound healing. Additionally, H10P10000NPs could be obtained via a simple, green and mild procedure in mass production, avoiding harsh conditions [24], tedious steps [58] or complicated purification procedure [54,58]. Hence, H10P10000NPs were promising antibacterial nano agent.

A kind of polymer nanoparticles crosslinked by PEI and HQ were prepared and reasonably optimized for the therapy of bacteria infected wound. The optimal polymer nanoparticles, i.e. H10P10000NPs, could selectively kill the Gram-positive but not -negative bacteria with the enhancing antibacterial activity compared to its source material (PEI and HQ). This may be explained as the synergistic effect of the selective antibacterial activity of HQ and well-known antibacterial activity of PEI. Interestingly, owing to its green fluorescence, H10P10000NPs could selectively stain Gram-positive but not -negative bacteria, which would be appreciated for discrimination of Gram-positive and -negative bacteria. Additionally, the H10P10000NPs could effectively kill the bacteria in the biofilm community, and this activity added an extra merit of the material by decreasing emergence of drug resistance. Furthermore, as expected, H10P10000NPs could effectively scavenge free radicals, which may protect the normal physiological metabolites such as protein and amino acid from excessive reactive oxygen species in the wound area and further alleviate the inflammation response. In the application of H10P10000NPs to a mouse wound infected by Staphylococcus aureus, the accelerated wound healing without obvious biotoxicity and side effects was obtained.

Compared with currently used antibacterial nanomaterials, the proposed H10P10000NPs could be designed and synthesized in large quantities via a safe and simple approach. H10P10000NPs possessed high permeability to bacteria with high biocompatibility, exerting multiple activities as selective antibacterial, antibiofilm and antioxidant activities superior to single antibacterial activity, and could also selectively stain Gram-positive bacteria. The present study paved a simple and green way to construct the “all-in-one” nano-antibacterial agent, holding the promise to overcome the drug resistance associated with the classical antibiotics with enhanced antibacterial efficacy.
